summary: Asparouhova applies Jane Jacobs' framework from 'The Death and Life of Great American Cities' to understand online public life. She distinguishes between technical privacy (keeping data secure) and social privacy (the norm of not prying into others' lives). Jacobs' concept of 'sidewalk life' — meaningful public interactions between strangers who respect each other's boundaries — maps directly onto social platforms like Twitter and Facebook. Healthy sidewalk life requires mutual trust, public characters who facilitate connections without violating privacy, and local context. When sidewalk life breaks down, people retreat to private circles, losing the serendipity and democratic mixing that public life offers. The essay argues we must reclaim online public life rather than abandon it for private enclaves.
key_points: Privacy is not just a technical problem (encryption, data security) but a social norm — the expectation that others shouldn't want to pry, even when they technically can
Jacobs' 'sidewalk life' — public interactions between strangers who respect mutual boundaries — is the key to thriving cities and maps directly onto social media platforms
Public characters (storekeepers, social media figures) serve as trusted nodes who facilitate community without violating personal boundaries, and they appoint themselves
When public life breaks down into 'togetherness or nothing,' people retreat to private cliques, losing the serendipity, democracy, and opportunity of urban encounters
Resurfacing old social media posts illustrates the tension between technical publicity and social privacy — data being public doesn't make snooping socially acceptable
money_quotes: Privacy is about more than who can peek into your life; it's a social norm about who should.

Unlike our physical environment, our online world contains layers of our past, present, and future selves, all occupying the same timespace. We are all time travelers, navigating multiple realities at any given moment.
If our smaller online communities (like private group chats or messaging threads) are our towns, social platforms (like Facebook, Twitter, or Instagram) are our cities.
Such relationships can, and do, endure for many years, for decades; they could never have formed without that line, much less endured.
We know that he combines a feeling of good will with a feeling of no personal responsibility about our private affairs.
I have lost the advantage of living in the city without getting the advantages of living in the suburbs.
It solves easy problems for an easy kind of population. So far as I have been able to discover, it fails to work, however, even on its own terms, with any other kind of population.
We can reclaim a public life where ideas are freely shared with one another, while our need for privacy is respected.
key_insight: Healthy online public life requires not just technical privacy protections but social privacy norms — the shared expectation that we won't pry into each other's lives — which enable the 'sidewalk life' of meaningful exchanges between strangers.
